Evaluation_Raw: Health Economic Assessment with Scenario Analysis
ICER_Raw: SEK 850,000 - 3.2 million/QALY vs Spinraza + BSC or SEK 2.9–5.7 million/QALY vs BSC alone
Main_Uncertainty: Very high uncertainty about long-term effects, how long the treatment effect lasts and uncertainty due to model based on muldtiple linked data sources
Conditions: Patients with 5q SMA with biallelic SMN1 mutation and either a clinical diagnosis of SMA type 1, or up to 3 copies of the SMN2 gene. TLV's economic scenarios mainly apply to symptomatic SMA type 1. No conclusions or results were presented for presymptomaic patients or symptomatic SMA type 2/3 due to uncertaintly in relative effectiveness.
Notes: Health Economic Assessment completed by TLV - no base-case scenario established for some patient groups due to uncertainty
Comments: TLV presents multiple scenario analyses wather than one base case for symptomatic SMA type 1. No results are presented for presymptomatic patients or symptomatic SMA type 2/3 because TLV considers relative effectiveness vs Spinraza too uncertain. Patient follow-up is considered important.
ICER_Basis_Note (Sweden only): range (vs Spinraza+BSC: 0.85-3.2M; vs BSC alone: 2.9-5.7M)

Evaluation_Raw: Clinical Value assessment + health economic assessment - Cost Minimization Approach
Decision_Date_Raw: Original recommendation: 26 May 2021; follow-up maintained: 23 Aug 2023; replaced by SMA guidance: 31 Aug 2023
ICER_Raw: No ICER reported. Medicinrådet used cost-minimisation because Zolgensma and nusinersen were considered clinically equivalent. Costs per patient were expected to equalise after 8–15 years, with substantial uncertainty; using AIP, equalisation was around 9 years
Main_Uncertainty: Long-term effect and long-term safety of one-time Zolgensma treatment. Uncertainty about how long nusinersen treatment would continue. Uncertainty around when costs equalize.
Conditions: Infants with SMA type 1 treated before 6 months old and not in permanent ventilation >16h/day, also presymptomatic infants born with SMA. Switching from comparator may be considered only in special cases if the child weights <13.5kg, is not severely muscle-weak and can breathe independently.
Notes: Medicinrådet considered Zolgensma clinically equivalent to nusinersen overall. The applicant’s full health economic model was only used to a limited extent because assumptions about effect, disease progression and mortality were not sufficiently supported by data. Medicinrådet instead used a simplified cost-minimisation approach
Comments: Zolgensma was not recommended in combination with nusinersen. The recommendation did not mean Zolgensma should automatically be first choice for all patients, switching from well-functioning nusinersen generally requires a specific clinical reason

Evaluation_Raw: Simplified method assessment: effect, safety and cost assessment + managed-access negotiation
Decision_Status_Raw: Introduced by Beslutningsforum for treatment of SMA - currently under reassessment because updated follow-up documentation was not submitted on time
Decision_Date_Raw: Final introduction: 25 Oct 2021; reassessment request: 19 Jan 2026; deadline extended to 15 Jun 2026 on 27 Apr 2026
ICER_Raw: No standard ICER/cost per QALY reported. Cost comparison only; Zolgensma had a very high one-time cost, around NOK 27 million before possible discounts
Main_Uncertainty: Sparse evidence base, short follow-up, major uncertainty about long-term effect, uncertainty about comparison with Spinraza and long-term costs
Conditions: Infants <6 months with manifest 5q SMA type 1 and biallelic SMN1 mutation, without clear symptoms at birth/type 0 and without ventilation support >16 h/day, presymptomatic infants <6 months with biallelic SMN1 mutation and 2 or 3 SMN2 copies, elected 6–18 month children <13.5 kg may switch from Spinraza in special cases if not severely weak and able to breathe independently, <6 month patients already started on Spinraza before Zolgensma availability could convert
Notes: Simplified HTA assessing effect, safety and costs. Zolgensma was compared with Spinraza. Final introduction was possible after negotiations and a result-based price agreement
Comments: First result-based agreement for a medicine in Norway. Treatment centralised at Oslo University Hospital, registry follow-up required, no combination with Spinraza or other disease-modifying treatment. In 2026, caution advised for new patients pending reassessment

Evaluation_Raw: Reimbursement / Subvention Assessment with Cost-Utility Analysis, later restricted subvention after side agreement
Comparator_Raw: BSC / no addition to BSC; tablet compared with Evrysdi oral solution
ICER_Raw: 2021: SEK 2.3–3.1M/QALY for SMA type 1; SEK 7.9M/QALY for SMA type 2/3. 2025: SEK 4.9M/QALY for SMA type 1; approx. SEK 800k/QALY for SMA type 2/3 after side agreement
Main_Uncertainty: Long-term effect, survival, QoL, model uncertainty, transferability to Swedish population, personal assistance costs
Conditions: 5q SMA type 1, type 2 or type 3. Tablet: patients >2 years and ≥20 kg under same restriction
Notes: Initial rejection due to costs not being reasonable vs benefit, later approval enabled by side agreement between Roche and regions
Comments: Important reversal case: price agreement changed outcome. SMA type 1 included via need-solidarity principle, tablet decision was a formulation extension
ICER_Basis_Note (Sweden only): range across 2021 and 2025 reassessments, by SMA subtype

Evaluation_Raw: Clinical Value Assessment + health economic assessment - later integrated into SMA treatment guidance and drug recommendation
Decision_Status_Raw: 2021: Recommended for selected SMA patients. 2024: Recommendation expanded after reassessment based on new price and new data. 2026: Evrysdi placed as first-choice option in current SMA drug recommendation
Decision_Date_Raw: Original recommendation: 27/10/2021. Reassessment/expanded recommendation: 28/08/2024. Current guidance: 29/04/2026
Comparator_Raw: Nusinersen for SMA type 1 and selected SMA type 2/3 groups; BSC/placebo/no medical treatment for groups where nusinersen was not standard care
ICER_Raw: No ICER reported. Medicinrådet used incremental cost and budget impact analyses. In 2021, risdiplam was less costly than nusinersen in recommended groups; list-price analyses showed high incremental costs vs BSC in older/non-recommended groups
Main_Uncertainty: Sparse data for SMA type 1, short follow-up,  uncertain effect in older children/adults in original assessment,  uncertainty around long-term survival, treatment duration and accumulated medicine costs
Conditions: 2021: SMA type 1 patients not in permanent ventilation >16 h/day; SMA type 2/3 patients under 6 years at treatment start. Later expanded to patients meeting fixed start/stop criteria. Current guidance includes Evrysdi as first choice for SMA type 1/presymptomatic infants, SMA type 2/3 children, young people and adults, including adults >25 years meeting criteria
Notes: Risdiplam was initially recommended because it was considered as good as nusinersen for relevant younger patients and less costly. Older SMA type 2/3 patients were not included in 2021 due to insufficient evidence. Later evidence and pricing supported broader use
Comments: Important expansion case: access broadened over time through new data, new price and integration into SMA pathway guidance. Evrysdi is oral and taken at home, unlike nusinersen. Combination therapy with other SMA medicines is not recommended

Evaluation_Raw: Multiple Evaluations: Simplified HTA (ID2021_088), Full HTA (ID2020_104), indication extensions and pricing reviews (ID2023_089, ID2025_010)
Comparator_Raw: Nusinersen (Spinraza) for children already eligible for treatment; supportive care/no disease-modifying treatment for adults and SMA type 3b populations
Reason_Text: Clinical evidence uncertainty + Comparator/positioning issue + Price / cost-effectiveness
ICER_Raw: Full HTA (ID2020_104) estimated approximately NOK 12 million per QALY gained versus supportive care. No ICER reported in simplified assessments.
Main_Uncertainty: Limited long-term evidence, small patient populations, uncertainty regarding long-term effectiveness and durability of treatment effect, particularly in adults
Conditions: Initially approved for children ≥2 months with SMA type 1, 2 or 3a. Later expanded to infants <2 months and subsequently to broader adult populations. National start/stop criteria apply.
Notes: Norway used different evaluation pathways depending on the patient population. Children already eligible for Spinraza underwent a simplified assessment, while adults and type 3b patients underwent a full HTA.
Comments: Market access was achieved through gradual indication expansion. Adult access required additional assessment and price negotiations. Tablet formulation was approved through a suitability assessment and pricing review rather than a new HTA.

Evaluation_Raw: Health Economic Assessment with Scenario Analysis
Comparator_Category: Best supportive care / no active treatment
ICER_Raw: 2017: SMA I SEK 5.7–7.7M/QALY; SMA II SEK 7.3–12.8M/QALY. 2023: Pediatric SMA I SEK 4.1–7.3M/QALY; Pediatric SMA II SEK 4.8–7.7M/QALY
Main_Uncertainty: Long-term survival, durability of treatment effect, quality-of-life estimates, disease progression modelling and limited evidence for adult SMA populations
Conditions: Patients with 5q SMA type I, II and III. Reassessment included pediatric SMA type I/II and adult SMA type II/III populations
Notes: Initial TLV assessment followed by major reassessment using long-term evidence and real-world data
Comments: Spinraza became the reference therapy against which both Zolgensma and Evrysdi were later assessed in Sweden. Reassessment demonstrated improved evidence base but uncertainty remained for adult SMA populations.
ICER_Basis_Note (Sweden only): range across 2017 and 2023 reassessments, by SMA subtype

Evaluation_Raw: Repeated HTA and reimbursement reassessments (2017, 2018, 2020) followed by national treatment guideline priorisation (2023)
Decision_Status_Raw: Restricted Recommendation / Recommended only for defined SMA subgroups; Not recommended for general SMA population
Comparator_Raw: BSC (initial assessments) + later compared directly with risdiplamn and onasemnogene in treatment guideline reviews
Reason_Text: Administrative/procedural + Clinical evidence uncertainty + Comparator/positioning issue + Price / cost-effectiveness
ICER_Raw: No ICER publicly reported in Medicinrådet recommendations; decisions based on clinical value relative to negotiated price
Main_Uncertainty: Limited long-term efficacy data, particularly for SMA type 2 and type 3; uncertainty regarding treatment effect in older children, adults and patients with longer disease duration; lack of evidence for SMA type 3 and adults
Conditions: Recommended for presymptomatic infants with 2–4 SMN2 copies, SMA type 1 and selected SMA type 2 patients meeting strict clinical criteria (including early treatment initiation and limited disease duration). Not recommended for SMA type 3 or general SMA populations.
Notes: Multiple reassessments due to new evidence and price negotiations. Medicinrådet repeatedly concluded that the price remained disproportionately high relative to documented benefit but accepted restricted use based on disease severity and demonstrated benefit in selected populations.
Comments: Illustrates Denmark’s highly restrictive access approach for rare disease therapies. Access expanded gradually as evidence accumulated, but recommendations remained limited to subgroups with strongest evidence. In 2023 Spinraza became a second-line option behind Evrysdi in most paediatric populations.

Evaluation_Raw: Rapid HTA (2017), multiple reassessments (2018, 2023) and price negotiations
Comparator_Category: Best supportive care / no active treatment
ICER_Raw: Initial HTA estimated ICERs ranging from approximately NOK 16–40 million per QALY in the Norwegian Medicines Agency base-case scenarios, depending on SMA subtype.
Main_Uncertainty: Long-term effectiveness, durability of treatment effect, limited trial data, uncertainty in health-economic model, effectiveness in older patients and later disease stages
Conditions: Restricted to defined paediatric SMA populations under strict start/stop criteria, national expert review, registry participation and regular reassessment. Expanded in 2023 to include SMA type 3b children.
Notes: Initially rejected because price was considered unacceptable and evidence uncertain. Following substantial price negotiations, Spinraza was introduced under one of the most restrictive managed-access schemes in Norway.
Comments: Illustrates Norway’s use of managed access, price negotiation, registry-based monitoring and start/stop criteria to manage uncertainty for an ultra-rare disease therapy.

Evaluation_Raw: FINOSE Joint Health Economic Assessment + NT-rådet recommendation
Decision_Date_Raw: FINOSE HTA February 2022; NT-rådet recommendation November 2023
Comparator_Category: Best supportive care / no active treatment
ICER_Raw: Approx. NOK 3.15–3.23 million/QALY in FINOSE scenarios; combined scenario approx. NOK 6.36 million/QALY
Main_Uncertainty: Very small patient population, no randomized comparator, uncertainty regarding long-term durability and safety
Conditions: Late infantile or early juvenile MLD; presymptomatic patients or early symptomatic patients still able to walk independently and before cognitive decline
Notes: Joint FINOSE assessment with Norway and Finland
Comments: Sweden became the operational treatment hub for the Nordic region
ICER_Basis_Note (Sweden only): source quotes NOK 3.15-6.36M in the Sweden record - likely a currency labelling error in source data, excluded pending verification

Evaluation_Raw: Health Economic Assessment with Scenario Analysis + NT-rådet recommendation
ICER_Raw: TLV scenario: Hemgenix can be dominant if effect is sustained over model horizon, but uncertainty remains high; NT-rådet concluded it was not cost-effective after failed price reduction negotiation
Main_Uncertainty: High scientific uncertainty and very high health-economic uncertainty; durability of one-time gene therapy effect unknown; no direct comparative study versus FIX prophylaxis; effect advantage versus current well-functioning treatment unclear
Conditions: Adults with severe or moderately severe hemophilia B without history of FIX inhibitors
Notes: NT-rådet recommended regions not to use Hemgenix. Negotiation did not produce sufficient cost reduction, and even an outcome-based agreement was judged insufficient
Comments: Clear negative access case. Current Swedish FIX prophylaxis is considered well-functioning, reducing disease severity and making the acceptable price for gene therapy harder to justify.
ICER_Basis_Note (Sweden only): no ICER reported; TLV scenario-dependent (dominant-to-uncertain)

Evaluation_Raw: Medicinrådet recommendation + reassessment + Amgros alternative price agreement
ICER_Raw: No public ICER. 2023 analysis found Hemgenix could be dominant at AIP/SAIP but this was highly uncertain and dependent on long response duration. 2024 recommendation accepted costs as reasonable after alternative price agreement
Main_Uncertainty: Long-term durability, sparse long-term safety data, uncertainty about how many patients will need to restart FIX prophylaxis
Conditions: Adults with severe or moderately severe hemophilia B without history of FIX inhibitors
Notes: Initial rejection due to price and uncertainty; later recommendation after new agreement where payment is linked to continued effect
Comments: Denmark is the positive contrast to Sweden: same core uncertainty, but access achieved through risk-sharing/payment-linked agreement.

Comparator_Raw: Standard FIX prophylaxis (factor IX replacement therapy)
ICER_Raw: Full cost-effectiveness analysis conducted; ICER not publicly available due to confidential pricing
Main_Uncertainty: Long-term durability of FIX expression; uncertainty regarding duration of treatment effect; extrapolation beyond trial follow-up
Conditions: Price must be equal to or lower than negotiated price; treatment centralized at Oslo University Hospital
Notes: DMP performed full HTA assessing severity, benefit, resource use, uncertainty and budget impact. Final approval followed price negotiations by Sykehusinnkjøp.
Comments: Major contrast with Sweden. Norway ultimately approved gene therapy after centralized negotiations and national funding.

Evaluation_Raw: Health Economic Assessment with Scenario Analysis + NT-rådet wait recommendation
Decision_Status_Raw: Not recommended / wait; not marketed in Sweden
Comparator_Raw: Prophylactic FVIII concentrates; Hemlibra considered in model
ICER_Raw: TLV base scenario: Roctavian dominant versus FVIII prophylaxis, meaning more effective and lower cost, but only under uncertain assumptions about duration of effect and long-term cost offsets
Main_Uncertainty: Very high uncertainty around durability of gene therapy effect, declining FVIII activity over time, representativeness of GENEr8-1 patients for Swedish well-controlled patients, HRQoL assumptions and lack of long-term data
Conditions: Adults with severe hemophilia A without history of FVIII inhibitors and without detectable AAV5 antibodies
Notes: TLV completed the health economic assessment, but NT-rådet could not proceed to final recommendation/contracting because BioMarin stated Roctavian would not be marketed in Sweden
Comments: Important “non-launch after HTA” case. Unlike Skysona, Sweden completed the HTA, but market access stopped because the company did not commercialize the medicine in Sweden.
ICER_Basis_Note (Sweden only): no ICER reported; TLV scenario-dependent (dominant under uncertain assumptions)
